title: Neural Permutation Learning: From Gumbel-Sinkhorn to ShuffleSoftSort

learningObjectives.description: Explain why naive permutation sorting is non-differentiable (discrete argmax) and describe how Gumbel-Sinkhorn produces a differentiable soft permutation via doubly stochastic matrix relaxation
Distinguish SoftSort (differentiable 1D sort) from ShuffleSoftSort (high-dimensional extension) in terms of memory complexity and applicability to 59-dimensional Gaussian attribute vectors
Explain how ShuffleSoftSort uses random projections to reduce the O(N²) Gumbel-Sinkhorn memory problem to O(N) parameters and maintain differentiability for joint training
Describe how joint optimization of ShuffleSoftSort and 3DGS training via a differentiable grid smoothness loss improves compression results compared to sorting a pre-trained model post-hoc

content: ## Sorting as Permutation Matrix Multiplication Sorting \(n\) elements is equivalent to multiplying by a **permutation matrix** \(P\): \[x^\top = [5, 1, 3, 4] \xrightarrow{P} [1, 3, 4, 5]\] \(P\) is a binary matrix with exactly one 1 per row and column. For \(n=4\), there are \(4! = 24\) possible permutation matrices. For \(n = 65536\) Gaussians, there are \(65536!\) — we need gradient-based optimization to search this space. **The problem**: permutation matrices are discrete (entries are 0 or 1), so the function \(P \mapsto \text{sort quality}(Px)\) has zero gradient almost everywhere. We cannot apply backpropagation directly. ## Question 1: Handling Non-Differentiability ### Gumbel-Sinkhorn The Sinkhorn operator iteratively normalizes a matrix to be doubly stochastic (rows and columns sum to 1): \[\text{Sinkhorn}(A) = \lim_{k\to\infty} (\text{col-normalize} \circ \text{row-normalize})^k (\exp(A/\tau))\] The result \(P_{soft}\) is a **soft permutation**: a doubly stochastic matrix that approximates a permutation matrix as temperature \(\tau \to 0\). - **Forward pass**: use \(P_{soft}\) with smooth entries — differentiable - **To get a hard permutation**: convert \(P_{soft}\) to hard via row-wise argmax or linear assignment - **Cost**: \(O(N^2)\) memory (the full \(N \times N\) matrix), and iterative normalization is slow ## Question 2: Permutation Representations How many parameters does each representation require? | Representation | Parameters | Notes | |---|---|---| | Discrete indices | \(N\) | Non-differentiable | | Low-rank factorization | \(2Nm\) (\(m \ll N\)) | Hard to optimize | | Full-rank (Gumbel-Sinkhorn) | \(N^2\) | Differentiable, expensive | | SoftSort (1D) | \(N\) | Differentiable but 1D only | | ShuffleSoftSort | \(N\) | Differentiable, generalizes to HD | ## Question 3: Network Architecture Two approaches: - **No network**: directly optimize the \(N^2\) weights of \(P_{soft}\). Flexible but quadratic memory. - **Transformer network**: input the data to be sorted alongside learnable parameters. The network produces permutation-like outputs conditioned on data. More parameter-efficient but adds architectural complexity. ## SoftSort: Differentiable 1D Sorting SoftSort (Prillo & Eisenschlos, ICML 2020) computes a differentiable sort of a 1D vector \(x\): \[P_{soft,ij} = \text{softmax}\!\left(-\frac{|x_i - \text{sort}(x)_j|}{\tau}\right)\] This creates a soft assignment between input positions and sorted positions. For small \(\tau\), \(P_{soft}\) approaches a hard permutation. **Limitation**: only works for 1D data. Gaussian attributes are 59-dimensional — we cannot directly apply SoftSort. ## ShuffleSoftSort: High-Dimensional Extension ShuffleSoftSort (Barthel et al., EUSIPCO 2025) extends SoftSort to high-dimensional data with only \(N\) parameters: 1. **Random shuffle**: randomly permute the \(N\) HD data points. 2. **Project to 1D**: apply SoftSort to a 1D projection of the shuffled data. 3. **Reverse shuffle**: undo the shuffling on the output permutation. 4. **Compute loss** on the resulting 2D grid arrangement. 5. **Repeat** with different random shuffles; each iteration refines the permutation from a different angle. **Why random shuffles help**: each shuffle exposes different pairwise relationships to SoftSort. No single 1D projection can capture all structure in 59D space, but many shuffles together build up a rich understanding of the data topology. **Advantages**: - Only \(N\) parameters (one sort key per data point) - Differentiable end-to-end - No \(N^2\) matrix — constant memory overhead - Can be jointly optimized with 3DGS training (the sort is differentiable, so gradients flow back into Gaussian attributes) ### Joint Optimization with 3DGS The most powerful application: train 3DGS and the sorting permutation simultaneously. The sorting loss \(\mathcal{L}_{sort}\) penalizes poorly-sorted grids, while the standard photometric loss \(\mathcal{L}_{render}\) maintains rendering quality. The total loss: \[\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{render} + \lambda_{sort}\mathcal{L}_{sort}\] This encourages Gaussian attributes to take values that, when sorted, produce smooth 2D grids — a form of coding-aware training that optimizes for downstream compressibility.

terms.name: Input vector (row)
terms.desc: The unsorted sequence of n values written as a row vector. Each entry occupies a position index 1…n.

terms.name: Permutation matrix
terms.desc: A binary n×n matrix with exactly one 1 per row and per column. Multiplying x by P reorders its elements. There are n! valid permutation matrices — one per possible ordering.

terms.name: Apply permutation
terms.desc: The action of P on x. The 1 in row i of P selects which element of x moves to output position i. The result is a reordered copy of x — no values are changed, only their positions.

terms.name: Temperature-scaled exponentiation
terms.desc: Converts the raw score matrix A into positive values. Temperature τ controls sharpness: small τ makes entries near 0 or 1 (approaching a hard permutation); large τ produces a nearly uniform doubly stochastic matrix.

terms.name: Row normalization
terms.desc: Divides each row by its sum, making every row sum to 1. Ensures each input element is 'assigned' to output positions with total weight 1.

terms.name: Column normalization
terms.desc: Divides each column by its sum, making every column sum to 1. Ensures each output position receives total assignment weight 1.

terms.name: Iterative convergence
terms.desc: Alternating row and column normalization is repeated until convergence. Sinkhorn's theorem guarantees this converges to a unique doubly stochastic matrix — the closest permutation-like matrix to exp(A/τ).

terms.name: Soft permutation matrix
terms.desc: The doubly stochastic output — entries are in (0,1) but rows and columns sum to 1, like a probabilistic assignment. As τ→0, P_soft approaches a hard permutation matrix and gradients vanish; as τ→∞, it approaches a uniform matrix.

terms.name: Soft assignment weight
terms.desc: How strongly input position i is assigned to sorted output position j. Sums to 1 across j for each i (softmax), making this a soft permutation row.

terms.name: Softmax over output positions
terms.desc: Converts the negative distances to a probability distribution over sorted positions. The position j whose sorted value is closest to x_i receives the highest weight.

terms.name: Distance to sorted target
terms.desc: How far input value x_i is from the j-th sorted value. Small distance means x_i 'belongs' near sorted position j. The negative sign in the softmax turns this into a similarity.

terms.name: Temperature
terms.desc: Controls how peaked the assignment is. Small τ → near-hard assignment (x_i maps almost entirely to its correct sorted position). Large τ → soft, spread-out assignment with smoother gradients but less accurate sorting.

terms.name: Total training loss
terms.desc: The combined objective minimized during joint 3DGS + permutation training. Gradients flow simultaneously into Gaussian attributes and the sort key, so the scene is encouraged to be both visually accurate and efficiently sortable.

terms.name: Photometric rendering loss
terms.desc: The standard 3DGS loss — typically L1 + D-SSIM between rendered and ground-truth images. Ensures the Gaussian representation stays faithful to the training photographs.

terms.name: Sorting loss weight
terms.desc: A scalar hyperparameter balancing rendering quality against grid smoothness. Too large: attributes distort to be sortable but rendering degrades. Too small: sorting quality improves little. Tuned per scene.

terms.name: Grid smoothness loss
terms.desc: Penalizes large differences between neighboring cells in the 2D grid after sorting. Encourages Gaussian attributes to take values that, when arranged spatially, vary smoothly — making the resulting attribute images more compressible.

doubly-stochastic.title: Doubly Stochastic Matrices & Permutation Relaxation
doubly-stochastic.body:
A matrix \(P \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}\) is **doubly stochastic** if all entries are non-negative and every row and every column sums to 1. Permutation matrices are the special case with binary entries.

The **Birkhoff–von Neumann theorem** states that the set of doubly stochastic matrices is exactly the **convex hull** of all permutation matrices — every doubly stochastic matrix is a weighted average of permutations. This makes doubly stochastic matrices the natural continuous relaxation for gradient-based permutation search: by optimizing over them via the differentiable Sinkhorn operator, we search the space of permutations without leaving a smooth, differentiable domain.

references.title: SoftSort: A Continuous Relaxation for the argsort Operator

references.label: Barthel et al. 2025
references.title: Permutation Learning with Only N Parameters: From SoftSort to Self-Organizing Gaussians (ShuffleSoftSort)
